1.title: An alphabet of grief
1.text: Chapters 1, 2, and 4 of Lamentations are acrostics: each has 22 verses, one for every letter of the Hebrew alphabet — a poetic way of saying the grief is complete, from A to Z.

1.question: Why has the Lord afflicted the city (Lamentations 1:5)?
options: For the multitude of her transgressions
Out of envy of the nations
For a broken vow of David

1.question: In Lamentations 1:12 the city calls to those who pass by to behold what?
options: If there is any sorrow like her sorrow

1.question: What does the city confess about the Lord in Lamentations 1:18?
options: The Lord is righteous, for she rebelled against his commandment

2.question: How does Lamentations 2:5 describe the Lord's dealings with Israel?
According to Lamentations 2:9, what is said of her prophets?
options: They find no vision from the Lord

2.question: In Lamentations 2:15, passersby mockingly recall that Jerusalem was called what?
options: The perfection of beauty, the joy of the whole earth

2.question: What does Lamentations 2:17 say the Lord has done?
options: Fulfilled his word that he commanded in days of old

2.title: When prophecy goes silent
2.text: Verse 9 mourns that 'the law is no more' and the prophets 'find no vision' — for an Israelite, losing the temple meant losing the very channels through which God had spoken.

3.question: How does the speaker describe himself in Lamentations 3:1?
options: The man that has seen affliction by the rod of God's wrath

3.question: In Lamentations 3:6, God set him in dark places, like whom?
To what two animals does Lamentations 3:10 compare the Lord's dealings?
options: A bear lying in wait and a lion

3.question: What did God fill the speaker with, in Lamentations 3:15?

3.title: Great is thy faithfulness
3.text: Lamentations 3:22-23 — 'his compassions fail not. They are new every morning' — became the refrain of the beloved hymn 'Great Is Thy Faithfulness.' The book's deepest hope sits at its very center.

3.question: Lamentations 3:27 says it is good for a man to bear what in his youth?
According to Lamentations 3:33, the Lord does not afflict the children of men how?
Lamentations 3:37 asks: who can speak and have it come to pass unless whom commands it?
Lamentations 3:39 asks why a living man should complain, given what?
What does Lamentations 3:40 urge the people to do?
options: Search their ways and turn again to the Lord

3.question: In Lamentations 3:48-49, what does the speaker say of his weeping eye?
From where did the speaker call upon the Lord's name in Lamentations 3:55?
In Lamentations 3:57, what did the Lord say when the speaker called?
How does the speaker close his plea about his enemies in Lamentations 3:64?
options: Asking the Lord to repay them according to their deeds

4.title: Purer than snow
4.text: Verse 7 recalls that Zion's princes (Nazarites) were once 'purer than snow, whiter than milk' — making the next verse's picture of them blackened by famine all the more shocking.

4.question: Lamentations 4:6 says the punishment of the people is greater than the sin of which city?
According to Lamentations 4:11, what did the Lord kindle in Zion?
options: A fire that devoured its foundations

4.question: Lamentations 4:13 blames the city's fall on the sins of her prophets and the iniquities of whom?
In Lamentations 4:21, to whom is it said the cup of judgment shall also pass?

5.question: How does Lamentations 5:3 describe the people?
In Lamentations 5:6, to which two nations did they give the hand for bread?
How does Lamentations 5:19 describe the Lord, in contrast to the ruined nation?
options: He remains for ever; his throne from generation to generation

5.title: An ending read twice
5.text: Because the book ends on a somber note, Jewish tradition repeats the hopeful plea 'Turn thou us unto thee, O Lord... renew our days as of old' — so the public reading ends on hope.
